reasoning:
The FRED statistical tool provided a naive primary anchor of 0.54 based on historical trend and seasonality. However, the SOFR Index is structurally designed as a continuous daily compounding of the SOFR overnight rate. Because the SOFR rate is substantially positive (currently reflecting Fed interest rates around ~4-5%), this index monotonically and strictly increases on every single business day. A decrease would only be possible under deeply negative US interest rates, which are not currently implemented or expected. Therefore, resolving 'YES' to the index having increased from its June 22 value is functionally guaranteed under normal financial operations. We update heavily from the naive 0.54 anchor towards near certainty. 

As explicitly instructed, uncertainty is set to increase with the forecast horizon, pulling the probabilities for more distant dates progressively closer to 0.5. Although the purely mathematical risk of a lower index value is practically zero across all horizons, longer horizons do marginally increase exposure to existential or systemic risks—such as the cessation of the SOFR index publication, catastrophic institutional disruptions, or a complete resetting/redefinition of FRED series ticker methodologies.
